Eczema.description: Eczema (excluding Atopic Dermatitis) includes contact dermatitis, nummular eczema, and dyshidrotic eczema. It causes itchy, inflamed, and sometimes blistering skin.
symptoms: Itchy skin
Dry, red, or brownish patches
Small raised bumps that may leak fluid
Crusted or scaly skin
Raw, sensitive skin from scratching
risk_factors: Exposure to chemical irritants at work
prevention: Wear protective gloves when handling chemicals
self_care: Apply a thick, fragrance-free moisturizer twice daily
Take short, lukewarm baths or showers
Use gentle, soap-free cleansers
when_to_consult_doctor: Symptoms do not improve with OTC creams
Skin is extremely painful or starts oozing yellow fluid
Itching disrupts sleep
emergency_signs: Fever with rapid spreading redness, warmth, or red streaks (secondary cellulitis)

faqs.a: No, eczema is an inflammatory skin reaction and cannot be spread from person to person.

faqs.q: What is the difference between eczema and atopic dermatitis?
faqs.a: Atopic dermatitis is a specific genetic, chronic form of eczema linked to allergies and asthma. Other eczemas may be temporary reactions to irritants.

Warts_Viral_Infections.description: Viral skin infections including Common Warts, Verrucas, and Molluscum Contagiosum, caused by viruses (such as HPV or Molluscum Contagiosum virus) invading the top layer of skin.
symptoms: Small, fleshy, grainy bumps
Rough, hard growths (warts)
Smooth, firm, dome-shaped bumps with a central dimple (molluscum)
Black pinpoints on warts (clotted blood vessels)
risk_factors: Using public showers or swimming pools barefoot
prevention: Avoid touching other people's warts or bumps
Wear sandals in public showers and pool areas
Do not share towels, socks, or shoes
self_care: Keep growths clean and dry
Use OTC salicylic acid treatments for warts (avoid on face)
Cover warts with a bandage to prevent spreading
when_to_consult_doctor: Growths are painful, bleeding, or changing color
Growths appear on the face or genitals
Multiple new lesions appear rapidly
emergency_signs: Severe spreading infection or redness around a lesion accompanied by high fever

faqs.a: Many warts go away on their own within 1-2 years, but treatments can help speed up the process.
No, it is a benign (harmless) viral infection, though it is highly contagious and spreads easily among children.

Melanoma.description: Melanoma is the most serious type of skin cancer. It develops in the melanocytes (melanin-producing cells) and can metastasize to other organs if not caught early.
symptoms: A mole that changes in size, shape, or color
Asymmetrical mole structure
Irregular, notched, or blurred borders
Multiple colors within one mole
Mole diameter larger than 6mm
Mole that bleeds, oozes, or feels itchy
causes: DNA damage to skin cells, primarily caused by ultraviolet (UV) radiation from sunlight or tanning beds
risk_factors: Fair skin, freckles, and light hair
Having many moles or atypical moles
prevention: Avoid direct sun exposure between 10 AM and 4 PM
self_care: Professional medical evaluation and surgical excision are required immediately. Self-care alone is unsafe.
when_to_consult_doctor: Any mole that matches the ABCDE criteria (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, Evolving)
A new pigmented growth on previously clear skin
emergency_signs: Rapidly growing, bleeding, or ulcerated dark lesion - seek urgent dermatology evaluation immediately.

faqs.a: Yes, if diagnosed and treated in its early stages, melanoma has a very high cure rate.
In men, it often appears on the trunk (chest/back). In women, it is most common on the legs. However, it can develop anywhere, including under fingernails or on palms.

Atopic_Dermatitis.description: Atopic Dermatitis is a chronic, genetic, inflammatory skin condition characterized by dry, extremely itchy skin, often associated with asthma and hay fever.
symptoms: Intense, persistent itching (especially at night)
Dry, sensitive skin
Red, inflamed patches on face, neck, elbows, and knees
Thickened, leathery skin from scratching
causes: Genetic mutations affecting the skin barrier (e.g., filaggrin gene)
Environmental triggers (dust mites, pollen, pet dander)
risk_factors: Family history of atopic dermatitis, asthma, or hay fever
Living in cold, dry, or polluted climates
prevention: Apply thick moisturizers immediately after bathing
self_care: Moisturize multiple times daily
Keep fingernails short to prevent scratching damage
Wear soft, breathable cotton clothing
when_to_consult_doctor: Itching interferes with sleep or daily activities
Signs of infection like yellow crusts, pus, or oozing
Symptoms worsen despite regular moisturizing
emergency_signs: Widespread painful blisters, skin peeling, and high fever (possible eczema herpeticum)

faqs.a: Yes, it strongly runs in families with a history of allergic conditions (the 'atopic triad' of eczema, asthma, and hay fever).

faqs.q: Does atopic dermatitis go away as children grow up?
faqs.a: Many children experience significant improvement or complete resolution of symptoms by adolescence, though some have lifelong dry, sensitive skin.

Basal_Cell_Carcinoma.description: Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) is the most common form of skin cancer. It grows slowly and rarely spreads to other parts of the body, but can cause localized tissue destruction if untreated.
symptoms: A pearly or waxy bump
A flat, flesh-colored or brown scar-like lesion
A bleeding or scabbing sore that heals and returns
A pink growth with slightly raised borders and a crusted center
causes: Long-term, cumulative exposure to ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the sun
prevention: Wear wide-brimmed hats and UV-blocking sunglasses
Avoid midday sun and outdoor tanning
self_care: Requires professional dermatologist diagnosis and surgical excision or treatment. Self-treatment is not possible.
when_to_consult_doctor: Any skin sore or growth that fails to heal within three to four weeks
A pearly bump that slowly enlarges
emergency_signs: Rapid bleeding or infected skin growth causing localized swelling and fever

faqs.a: It is rarely life-threatening because it grows very slowly and rarely metastasizes, but it must be removed to prevent damage to surrounding skin and bone.

faqs.q: What is the primary treatment for BCC?
faqs.a: Minor surgical excision, Mohs micrographic surgery, or cryotherapy, usually performed in a dermatologist's clinic.

Melanocytic_Nevi.description: Melanocytic Nevi, commonly known as moles, are benign, common growths on the skin caused by a cluster of melanocytes (pigment-producing cells).
symptoms: Symmetric, round or oval shape
Smooth, uniform color (tan, brown, or black)
Flat or slightly raised surface
Sharp, well-defined borders
Usually stable in size and appearance
causes: Proliferation of benign melanocytes in the skin layers
prevention: Sun protection helps prevent the formation of new moles and reduces the risk of malignant changes in existing moles.
self_care: Perform monthly checks to monitor for changes in size, shape, color, or symmetry.
when_to_consult_doctor: A mole becomes asymmetrical, develops irregular borders, exhibits multiple colors, grows larger than 6mm, or starts bleeding/itching

faqs.q: Can a benign mole turn into cancer?
faqs.a: Most benign moles never become cancerous, but a small percentage can transition into melanoma. Regular monitoring is key.

faqs.q: Is it safe to remove moles at home?
faqs.a: No, self-removal of moles can cause severe infections, scarring, and leaves no tissue for biopsy, which is critical to rule out cancer.

Benign_Keratosis.description: Benign Keratosis-like Lesions (BKL) comprise harmless skin growths including seborrheic keratosis, solar lentigines, and lichen planus-like keratosis, which often resemble skin cancers but are benign.
symptoms: Waxy, scaly, or 'stuck-on' appearance
Color ranging from light tan to dark brown or black
Round or oval shaped, often small but can grow to an inch or more
Flat or slightly raised rough surface
prevention: Protect skin from sun damage to limit solar lentigines and potential actinic growths.
self_care: Keep skin moisturized. Avoid picking or scratching the growths, which can cause bleeding or infection.
when_to_consult_doctor: Growths grow rapidly, bleed, or become inflamed
A growth looks dark, irregular, or has multiple colors (to rule out melanoma)

faqs.a: No, they are completely non-infectious aging/sun-related growths and cannot spread to others.

faqs.q: Do benign keratoses need to be removed?
faqs.a: No, they do not require treatment unless they become irritated by clothing or are cosmetically undesirable, in which case they can be frozen off.

Psoriasis_Lichen_Planus.description: This group includes Psoriasis (an autoimmune condition causing rapid skin cell buildup into thick, silvery scales) and Lichen Planus (an inflammatory condition causing itchy, purple, flat-topped bumps).
symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales (Psoriasis)
Dry, cracked skin that may bleed
Itching, burning, or soreness
Itchy, purple, flat-topped bumps, often on wrists or ankles (Lichen Planus)
Lacy white patches in the mouth
causes: Immune system dysfunction causing accelerated skin cell turnover or inflammatory attacks on skin/mucous membranes
risk_factors: Certain viral infections (Hepatitis C is linked to Lichen Planus)
prevention: Maintain a healthy lifestyle and avoid smoking
self_care: Moisturize daily with heavy creams
Take warm baths with Epsom salts or oatmeal
Limit alcohol intake
when_to_consult_doctor: Joint pain, stiffness, or swelling (indicative of psoriatic arthritis)
Widespread rash covering more than 10% of the body
Plaques cause severe pain or bleed easily
emergency_signs: Widespread redness and peeling skin over the entire body accompanied by chills and high fever (possible erythrodermic psoriasis)

faqs.a: Yes, up to 30% of people with psoriasis develop psoriatic arthritis, which causes joint pain and stiffness.

faqs.q: Is lichen planus a permanent condition?
faqs.a: Lichen planus on the skin usually resolves on its own within 1-2 years, though oral lichen planus can be chronic and require ongoing management.

Seborrheic_Keratoses.description: Seborrheic Keratoses are highly common, benign (non-cancerous) skin growths that appear as waxy, scaly, or 'stuck-on' spots. They are a normal part of the skin's aging process.
symptoms: Waxy, paste-like, or rough 'stuck-on' appearance
Colors ranging from yellow-tan to dark brown or black
Usually oval or round shape
Can appear on the face, chest, shoulders, or back
prevention: No known prevention methods as they are related to genetics and natural skin aging.
self_care: Avoid scratching or picking at the lesions, which can lead to bleeding, pain, and secondary infection.
when_to_consult_doctor: A growth changes rapidly, bleeds, or becomes highly irritated
A lesion is black or has a highly irregular border (to distinguish from melanoma)

faqs.a: No, seborrheic keratoses are completely benign and never turn into skin cancer.
Yes, dermatologists can easily remove them using cryotherapy (freezing), shave excision, or electrocautery.

Tinea_Fungal_Infections.description: Fungal skin infections (Tinea) caused by dermatophyte fungi, including ringworm, athlete's foot (tinea pedis), jock itch (tinea cruris), and tinea versicolor.
symptoms: Red, itchy, circular rash with raised, scaly edges (ringworm)
Cracked, peeling, itchy skin between the toes (athlete's foot)
Red, itchy, burning rash in the groin area (jock itch)
Discolored skin patches (lighter or darker) on the chest or back
causes: Dermatophyte fungi infecting the dead keratin layers of skin
Sharing clothing, towels, or bedsheets with an infected person
risk_factors: Using public gyms, showers, or locker rooms without footwear
prevention: Keep skin clean and dry, especially in skin folds
Wear flip-flops in public locker rooms
self_care: Apply OTC antifungal creams (clotrimazole, miconazole, terbinafine) daily as directed
Keep the infected area clean and dry
Wash towels and bedding in hot water
when_to_consult_doctor: Infection does not improve after 2 weeks of OTC treatment
Infection spreads to the scalp or nails
Redness spreads rapidly with severe pain
emergency_signs: Fever with spreading redness, warmth, swelling, or red streaks (secondary bacterial infection)

faqs.q: Is ringworm caused by a worm?
faqs.a: No, ringworm is a fungal infection of the skin. The name comes from the characteristic ring-like shape of the rash.

faqs.q: Can I catch a fungal infection from pets?
faqs.a: Yes, dogs and cats can carry ringworm and easily transmit it to humans through direct contact.

Healthy_Skin.description: No disease detected. Skin is healthy and functions normally.
self_care: Cleanse and moisturize skin twice daily
when_to_consult_doctor: If new rashes, spots, or changing moles develop in the future

faqs.q: How often should I check my skin?
faqs.a: A monthly self-exam of your skin is recommended to notice any changes early.
